main_dirt.bias_notes: Deep, sandy loam surface that tires horses out and requires high energy expenditure. Wire-to-wire is difficult in 6F sprints. The deep surface chews up one-dimensional speed in routes.
main_dirt.post_bias: Inside posts are a massive advantage at 1 1/8 miles due to the short run to the first turn, BUT beware of the 'Dead Rail' (1-2 path) in sprints where Post 1 is often a distinct disadvantage.

mellon_turf_outer.bias_notes: Wider sweeping turns. Fair and closer-friendly. Tactical speed or mid-pack stalkers tend to excel. Early speed generally struggles to go wire-to-wire here.
mellon_turf_outer.post_bias: Outside posts (7 and higher) historically perform disproportionately well on this outer course compared to the inner turf.

inner_turf.bias_notes: Features very tight, sharp turns. Distinctly SPEED FAVORING. Visually favors smaller, athletic horses that can corner well over large, long-striding bulky horses.
inner_turf.post_bias: The inside posts (1-4) are dominant and offer a significant advantage, especially at 1 mile and 1 1/16 miles.

handicapping_angles.algorithmic_flag: Downgrade 'Need-the-Lead' (E) types with low stamina on the dirt. Aggressively upgrade Stalkers/Pressers who can sit off the pace.
handicapping_angles.the_belmont_flip: Downgrade horses whose best speed figures came in 1-turn routes at Belmont. Upgrade true 2-turn route specialists coming from tracks like Churchill, Aqueduct, or past Saratoga meets.
handicapping_angles.late_meet_fade: As the meet progresses into August, the dirt rail often becomes 'dead' and cuppy, shifting the route bias heavily toward closers.
handicapping_angles.turf_trip_quality: On the grass, trip quality (saving ground, avoiding traffic) dictates winners more than raw speed figures. Wide-drawn closers in big fields are highly vulnerable.

Belmont_Park.bias_notes: Big Sandy. Large circumference means fair track. Closers can win from anywhere.

handicapping_angles.pace_scenario: Closers love Belmont. Even sprints allow come-from-behind wins.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A 1 1/8-mile oval notorious for being 'The Conveyor Belt'. The surface is hard, fast, and sandy, producing severe kickback. It frequently plays with a massive 'GOLDEN RAIL' bias. Pure early speed on the inside is incredibly dominant, especially in 6F sprints.
main_dirt.post_bias: Inside posts are gold in sprints IF the horse has the speed to clear. However, in the unique One-Turn Mile (run out of a chute), Post 1 is a severe disadvantage because horses get pinned inside; Posts 3-6 are ideal.

main_turf.bias_notes: A fast, flat turf course that requires tactical speed. Deep closers can win if the pace melts down, but 'ground-saving' trips hugging the rail are generally required to hit the board.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside to middle posts are preferred. Outside posts (8+) in two-turn turf routes (1 1/16 miles) are a massive disadvantage due to the extremely short run into the first turn.

tapeta.bias_notes: The inner synthetic track provides a perfectly fair, forgiving surface. Unlike the main dirt track, speed is highly vulnerable here. The Tapeta heavily rewards patient stalkers and deep closers ('Swoopers').
tapeta.post_bias: Very fair across the board. Outside posts are not heavily penalized as riders have plenty of time to find cover.

handicapping_angles.the_golden_rail_wire: In dirt sprints, heavily upgrade 'Need-the-Lead' (E) types drawn inside. If they miss the break, they lose, but if they clear early, they are nearly impossible to run down.
handicapping_angles.kickback_elimination: Because the kickback on the dirt is so severe, aggressively downgrade stalkers drawn inside (Posts 1-3) who lack early speed. They will get buried in flying sand and frequently stop running.
handicapping_angles.the_championship_meet_shift: Gulfstream is dynamically tiered. During the Winter Championship Meet (Dec-Mar), it is an elite Tier 1 track. During the Summer (Apr-Nov), it drops to a Tier 2 track. Adjust shipper class penalties heavily based on the season.
handicapping_angles.tapeta_turf_translators: When turf races are rained off onto the Tapeta, heavily upgrade horses with proven turf form over those with only dirt form. Grass mechanics translate beautifully to the synthetic.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A 1-mile dirt oval that historically plays extremely hard and fast. It frequently develops a 'Golden Rail' bias that heavily favors early speed ('E' types) and rail-skimming trips. Deep closers often struggle on the dirt unless there is a suicidal pace duel.
main_dirt.post_bias: Inside posts are advantageous in sprints, provided the horse has the gate speed to utilize the position. In two-turn routes, saving ground on the tight turns is critical.

main_turf.bias_notes: A flat, fast turf course that plays reasonably fair, though tactical speed is generally preferred over deep closing styles.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside to middle draws are optimal. Wide draws in two-turn turf routes are a severe penalty.

downhill_turf.bias_notes: One of the most unique layouts in America. Horses start at the top of the hill, run down a right-hand bend, cross the main dirt track, and finish on the main turf stretch. Agility and experience are paramount.
downhill_turf.post_bias: Outside posts are actually preferred here, as they allow horses a clear, unimpeded run down the hill before making the right-hand turn and dirt crossing.

handicapping_angles.downhill_specialists: The downhill turf course requires a highly specific skill set. Heavily upgrade 'Horses for Courses' who have proven they can handle the right-hand turn and the visual distraction of the dirt crossing.
handicapping_angles.the_west_coast_island: Southern California racing is highly insulated. Top-tier Santa Anita form is elite, but apply caution when California horses ship East (e.g., to Churchill or Belmont), as they often struggle to adapt to deeper, more tiring dirt surfaces.
handicapping_angles.golden_rail_watch: Always monitor the early dirt races. If wire-to-wire rail runners dominate the first few events, aggressively upgrade inside speed horses for the remainder of the card.

Del_Mar.bias_notes: Short stretch. Speed/Pressers favored on dirt. Turf is narrow and firm.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Speed has slight edge due to one-mile configuration.
handicapping_angles.turf_notes: Inside posts slight advantage on turf.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A standard 1-mile dirt oval with a generous 995-foot home stretch. While generally fair, it can occasionally develop a strong inside/speed bias depending on weather and track maintenance. The long stretch gives 'Swoopers' (closers) ample runway to catch tiring front-runners in two-turn route races.
main_dirt.post_bias: In 5.5f and 6f sprints, inside and middle posts are preferred, particularly for horses with early speed. In two-turn routes, inside posts (1-4) hold a tactical advantage for saving ground around the turns.

main_turf.bias_notes: Set inside the main track, this is a much tighter 7/8-mile (7 furlongs) oval. Because of the sharper turns and relatively short straightaway, tactical early speed and rail-skimming trips are absolutely critical. Pure closers frequently run out of ground.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside posts hold a massive advantage here. Outside draws (8+) in two-turn turf routes (like 1 1/16 miles) are severely punished due to the incredibly short run into the first turn.

handicapping_angles.turf_tactical_speed: On the grass, aggressively upgrade stalkers and pressers ('P' types) drawn inside. The tight 7-furlong configuration makes it incredibly difficult for deep closers to loop the field.
handicapping_angles.the_mid_atlantic_shipper: Delaware Park is a massive intersection for the Mid-Atlantic circuit. Form lines from Laurel Park, Parx, and Monmouth intersect here. Respect horses dropping out of tough Monmouth or Laurel allowance races into Delaware claimers.
handicapping_angles.the_golden_rail_watch: Always monitor the first few dirt races of the card. If wire-to-wire rail runners dominate, aggressively upgrade inside speed horses for the rest of the day, as DEL can quickly become a 'Golden Rail' speed highway.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A 1-mile oval featuring one of the longest home stretches in North America (1,234 feet). The dirt surface can vary heavily depending on weather, sometimes playing cuppy and tiring, and other times becoming a lightning-fast 'highway'. Because of the long stretch, deep closers have a significantly better chance here than at tight-turn tracks like Parx or Thistledown.
main_dirt.post_bias: In 1 Mile dirt races (run out of the one-turn chute), outside posts are generally fine. In two-turn routes (1 1/16 miles or longer), inside posts hold an advantage, as saving ground on both turns is crucial.

matt_winn_turf.bias_notes: Located inside the main track, this 7/8-mile oval is much tighter than the sprawling dirt course. Because the turns are sharper, tactical speed and ground-saving trips are vital. The turf course handles rain decently, but can develop a distinct 'outside paths' bias when heavily chewed up.
matt_winn_turf.post_bias: Inside posts are advantageous in 1 Mile and 1 1/16 mile turf routes due to the short run to the first turn.

handicapping_angles.horses_for_courses: Churchill Downs has one of the highest 'Horses for Courses' predictability ratings in the country. Heavily upgrade horses with proven winning form specifically over the Churchill dirt, as many horses simply refuse to run over this unique surface.
handicapping_angles.the_long_stretch_swoop: In route races, heavily downgrade front-runners ('E' types) who have a history of folding in the final furlong. The 1,234-foot stretch will completely expose horses lacking genuine stamina.
handicapping_angles.the_shipper_advantage: In undercard stakes and allowance races, respect horses shipping in from tough circuits like New York (Aqueduct/Saratoga) or Gulfstream Park, as they often outclass local Midwest horses.

Keeneland.bias_notes: Short stretch means speed can steal. Rail position critical.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Speed bias due to short stretch.
handicapping_angles.turf_notes: Excellent turf. Inside posts slight advantage.

Oaklawn_Park.bias_notes: Track plays fair - can develop speed or closer bias depending on maintenance.

Fair_Grounds.bias_notes: Deep, tiring track. Extremely long stretch. Closers have a fair chance.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Can be deep and tiring - stamina matters.
handicapping_angles.turf_notes: Wide turf course, favors late kicks.

Tampa_Bay_Downs.bias_notes: One-mile track but plays fairer than most. Sandy surface.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Fairer than typical one-mile track but speed still has edge.
handicapping_angles.turf_notes: Smaller turf but well-maintained. Inside posts advantage.

main_dirt.bias_notes: Historically a 'Golden Rail' track. The dirt surface is hard and fast, rewarding horses that can secure the lead or sit prominently on the rail.
main_dirt.post_bias: Posts 1-4 are consistently advantageous in sprints.

handicapping_angles.speed_bias: Aggressively favor front-runners and pressers. Deep closers are frequently neutralized.
handicapping_angles.one_turn_mile: The one-turn mile at Parx is a speed-dominant event. Inside speed is extremely dangerous here.
handicapping_angles.mid-atlantic_shippers: Be wary of horses shipping in from Laurel or Penn National; Parx often plays faster than those tracks.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A 1-mile dirt oval historically known for a massive and consistent early speed bias. The lightning-fast surface and relatively tight turns frequently favor 'need-the-lead' front-runners ('E' types). It is notoriously difficult for deep closers to make up ground on the dirt here unless a fierce pace meltdown occurs.
main_dirt.post_bias: In short sprints (5.5F to 6F), inside and middle posts are highly preferred. The combination of early speed and an inside rail draw is an incredibly deadly angle here. In two-turn dirt routes, inside posts (1-3) hold a massive advantage; outside posts (8+) are highly detrimental.

main_turf.bias_notes: A tighter 7/8-mile turf course. Because the turns are sharper and the layout is smaller than premium turf courses (like Belmont), tactical speed and ground-saving trips are highly rewarded. However, unlike the dirt, the turf course plays much fairer to stalkers and mid-pack closers.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside posts hold a strong advantage. Wide sweeps on this turf course often result in significant ground loss, and outside posts in turf routes should generally be downgraded.

handicapping_angles.the_speed_bias_highway: Never ignore pure early speed ('E' types) on the Monmouth dirt, even if they are stepping up in class. If a horse projects to be the lone speed on paper, heavily upgrade them.
handicapping_angles.aggressive_jockey_upgrade: Monmouth heavily rewards aggressive, speed-riding jockeys who immediately send their mounts to the front (e.g., local kingpin Paco Lopez). Upgrade horses with high early speed drawn inside with these types of riders.
handicapping_angles.the_shipper_advantage: Monmouth draws heavily from the New York and Florida circuits. Respect horses dropping out of tough Belmont/Saratoga allowances or Gulfstream Park winter meets, as they frequently outclass the local New Jersey-bred stalwarts.

Laurel_Park.bias_notes: Maryland's primary track. Fair track overall.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Generally fair but can develop speed bias.

main_tapeta.bias_notes: An all-weather Tapeta surface that plays much closer to turf than dirt. It is incredibly fair and highly penalizes one-dimensional front-runners. Sustained momentum and late closing kicks ('Swoopers') are heavily rewarded.
main_tapeta.post_bias: Extremely fair. Outside posts are not heavily penalized in sprint chutes because riders have time to find cover. Saving ground on the turns in routes is still mathematically beneficial.

ep_taylor_turf.bias_notes: A massive, sweeping European-style turf course with a grueling 1,440-foot home stretch. It is the ultimate closer's paradise. Pure speed horses almost always collapse in the final furlong.
ep_taylor_turf.post_bias: Very fair due to the massive circumference. Wide sweeps are entirely possible.

inner_turf.bias_notes: A standard North American-style turf oval sitting inside the main track. Considerably tighter than the E.P. Taylor course, placing a higher premium on tactical early speed and inside positioning.
inner_turf.post_bias: Inside posts hold an advantage here, unlike the outer turf course.

handicapping_angles.the_synthetic_shift: Heavily upgrade turf specialists when races are moved off the grass onto the Tapeta. Conversely, aggressively downgrade horses shipping in whose only good form is on traditional deep dirt tracks (like Saratoga or Parx), as dirt form frequently fails to translate to Tapeta.
handicapping_angles.the_endless_stretch: On the E.P. Taylor turf course, completely toss 'Need-the-Lead' types in route races. The 1,440-foot stretch will expose any horse lacking genuine stamina.
handicapping_angles.shipper_class_evaluation: Top-tier shippers from elite US turf hubs (like Belmont or Gulfstream) frequently perform exceptionally well here, but local Tapeta specialists hold a distinct home-court advantage on the main track.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A very tight 6.5-furlong oval. Because the track is smaller than standard 1-mile circuits, the turns are tight, which inherently creates a massive advantage for horses with early speed and inside positioning. Deep closers are at a severe disadvantage.
main_dirt.post_bias: Inside posts (1-4) hold a significant statistical advantage, particularly in sprint races. Horses drawn wide (7+) are frequently caught out wide on the tight turns and forced to cover far too much ground.

handicapping_angles.the_bullring_speed_bias: Aggressively upgrade pure front-runners ('E' types) and rail-skimming pressers. If a horse can clear the field early from an inside draw, they are incredibly difficult to run down on this tight layout.
handicapping_angles.the_century_mile_shipper_trap: Apply extreme caution to horses shipping in from Century Mile (Alberta). Century Mile is a massive, expansive 1-mile track. Large, long-striding closers who ran well at Century frequently struggle to navigate the tight, sharp turns at Assiniboia.
handicapping_angles.woodbine_class_droppers: Horses shipping from Woodbine down to Assiniboia are taking a massive class drop. However, do not blindly bet them if they lack tactical early speed; class alone cannot overcome being caught 5-wide on the turns here.

Kentucky_Downs.bias_notes: Unique European-style course with elevation changes. NO DIRT.

handicapping_angles.pace_scenario: Horses need to rate and handle the hills. Closers often thrive.

Colonial_Downs.bias_notes: Huge dirt track (1.25 miles), massive turf course. Very fair.

handicapping_angles.turf_notes: Secretariat Turf Course is widest in country.

Horseshoe_Indianapolis.bias_notes: Fairer than many regional ovals. Strong IN-bred program.

Hawthorne.bias_notes: Long stretch makes it one of the fairest one-mile tracks. Closers can get there.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: Fairer than typical one-mile due to long stretch.

Emerald_Downs.bias_notes: Pacific Northwest. Speed bias - inside posts advantage.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A massive 1 1/8-mile dirt oval. During the winter months, the track frequently develops a severe 'DEAD RAIL'. Jockeys will deliberately steer 3 to 4 paths wide looking for the better, faster ground. Outside flow and outside momentum are the dominant biases.
main_dirt.post_bias: Because of the winter dead rail, inside posts (1-2) can be a severe disadvantage in dirt sprints. Mid-to-outside draws are highly preferred so horses can immediately establish 'Outside Speed'.

outer_turf.bias_notes: A sweeping 1-mile turf course. Generally plays very fair to all run styles.
outer_turf.post_bias: Fair across the board, though saving some ground around the wide, sweeping turns is always beneficial.

inner_turf.bias_notes: A tighter 7/8-mile inner turf course. Because the turns are significantly sharper than the outer course, it distinctly favors tactical early speed and rail-skimming trips.
inner_turf.post_bias: Inside posts (1-4) hold a distinct advantage here due to the tighter geometry and shorter run into the first turn.

handicapping_angles.the_winter_dead_rail: During the winter meet, aggressively downgrade horses breaking from the inside rail unless they possess the tactical speed to immediately cross over and get 3-paths wide. Upgrade 'Outside Speed' over 'Rail Speed'.
handicapping_angles.the_seasonal_tier_shift: Aqueduct operates as a Tier 2 track in the harsh winter, but bumps up to a Tier 1.5 track in the Spring and Fall when elite New York stables return. Apply a heavier class penalty (+8 lengths) during the Spring/Fall meets than in the Winter (+6 lengths).
handicapping_angles.the_outside_flow: In route races on the dirt, horses that can secure a clear, wide stalking trip ('Outside Flow') frequently run down horses stuck battling on the slower inside rail.

Turf_Paradise.bias_notes: Desert track. Speed bias on dirt. Turf course is tight.

Sunland_Park.bias_notes: Border track. Speed bias. Sunland Derby is key prep.

Delta_Downs.bias_notes: THE BULLRING. 6-furlong oval. Extreme speed bias. Inside posts are critical.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: EXTREME speed bias. If you aren't in front or 2nd, you lose.

Penn_National.bias_notes: Night racing. Speed bias - inside posts advantage.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A standard 1-mile dirt oval with a moderately long 905-foot stretch. The surface can vary significantly depending on West Virginia weather, frequently becoming deep and tiring. Unlike tighter bullrings, the generous stretch gives closers a fair shot if the early pace is contested.
main_dirt.post_bias: Fair across the board in route races, but inside-to-middle draws are preferred in 5f and 6f dashes to establish early position.

main_turf.bias_notes: A tight 7/8-mile turf course inside the main dirt track. Because of the sharp turns and shorter stretch, tactical early speed and rail-skimming trips are absolutely critical. Pure closers ('Swoopers') are at a massive disadvantage here.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside posts (1-4) hold a massive advantage. Outside draws (8+) in turf routes are a severe penalty due to the incredibly short run to the first turn.

handicapping_angles.the_shipper_advantage: Mountaineer frequently draws horses dropping in class from tougher regional circuits (like Mahoning Valley, Thistledown, or even Presque Isle). Always upgrade horses shipping in from tougher allowance or claiming company.
handicapping_angles.turf_tactical_speed: On the grass, aggressively downgrade 'Need-the-Lead' front runners who have drawn outside, as they will burn too much energy crossing over. Heavily upgrade stalkers drawn inside who can save ground and pounce on the tight turn.
handicapping_angles.wet_weather_slogging: During the rainy season, the Mountaineer dirt track can become incredibly deep and heavy. Look for proven 'mudlarks' who have historically won on 'Off' tracks, and downgrade speed horses that tire late on fast tracks.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A standard 1-mile dirt oval with a 960-foot home stretch and chutes for 6-furlong and 1 1/4-mile races. The track relies entirely on dirt racing, and the surface can frequently develop a strong inside/speed bias, particularly during the dry summer months. Because the corners are relatively standard, early positioning is critical.
main_dirt.post_bias: In the standard 5f to 6f sprint dashes, inside and middle posts (1-4) hold a significant statistical advantage. Wide draws are punished unless the horse possesses enough explosive early speed to cross over before the first turn. In two-turn route races, saving ground on the inside is highly preferred.

handicapping_angles.the_downstate_shipper: Finger Lakes is the primary landing spot for horses dropping out of the ultra-competitive 'downstate' NYRA circuit (Aqueduct, Belmont, Saratoga). Aggressively upgrade horses shipping into Finger Lakes from NYRA tracks, as they typically hold a massive inherent class edge over the local stalwarts.
handicapping_angles.inside_speed_dominance: Particularly in sprints, pure early speed ('E' types) drawn on the inside rail are incredibly potent. If an inside-drawn horse projects to be the lone speed on paper, they are extremely difficult to run down.
handicapping_angles.weather_dependent_surface: As a track operating in upstate New York from spring through late autumn, the sandy loam surface can drastically change with precipitation. Monitor early races on wet days; if the rail becomes deep and tiring (a 'dead rail'), immediately upgrade outside stalkers.

main_dirt.bias_notes: A standard 1-mile dirt oval with absolutely no turf course. The track generally plays very fast and frequently develops a distinct inside speed bias, particularly in the 5F and 6F sprints. However, the generous 978-foot home straight ensures that if a suicidal early speed duel occurs, stalkers have plenty of room to grind down the leaders in route races.
main_dirt.post_bias: In sprints (5f to 6f), inside posts (1-3) hold a massive statistical advantage, particularly for horses with explosive gate speed. In route races, the post bias is far more balanced, though saving ground on the first turn is always mathematically preferred.

handicapping_angles.the_inside_speed_highway: Aggressively upgrade pure front-runners ('E' types) drawn on the inside rail in sprint races. If a horse can secure the rail early without being pressed, they are incredibly difficult to run down on this surface.
handicapping_angles.regional_class_droppers: Because Thistledown hosts a massive number of claiming races, 'class dropping' is a primary angle. Respect horses shipping in and dropping down from tougher allowance company at Presque Isle Downs, Churchill, or even Indiana Grand.
handicapping_angles.ohio_derby_invaders: For the track's marquee race (The Ohio Derby), heavily upgrade shippers coming from elite Tier 1 tracks (like Churchill Downs or Belmont). Local Ohio stalwarts frequently struggle with the massive step up in class when the national operations invade.

Belterra_Park.bias_notes: River downs. Speed favors on dirt. Small turf favors inside.

Royal_Randwick.bias_notes: Two tracks: Kensington (inner) and Course Proper. Course Proper is fair with 'The Rise'. Kensington favors on-pace.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Course Proper is fair. Kensington favors on-pace.

Albury.bias_notes: A spacious, flat regional track located on the NSW/VIC border. It features a generous 400m home straight, making it a premium and very fair galloping surface.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extremely fair. The long straight gives backmarkers plenty of time and room to wind up and swoop.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Generally fair across most distances due to the wide track.
handicapping_angles.border_form: Always cross-reference Victorian country form against local NSW form.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. Set inside the main Randwick course, the Kensington track utilizes an all-weather StrathAyr (sand base) construction. It features a somewhat narrow 18-metre width at the winning post. Because it drains exceptionally well, it often plays firmer and faster than the main track, frequently generating a strong on-pace and rail bias.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are highly advantageous across all sprint trips. Because the circuit is tighter than the Course Proper, being caught three-wide without cover here is significantly more punishing.

handicapping_angles.the_strathayr_speed_bias: When the track is rated Good, it frequently becomes a 'leader's track'. Heavily upgrade early speed ('E' types) and rail-skimming pressers. Deep closers ('S' types) often run out of time to catch the leaders despite the 401m straight.
handicapping_angles.the_wet_weather_mirage: Kenso handles rain better than almost any track in Sydney. While surrounding tracks might race as Heavy, Kenso often plays much better (e.g., Soft 6). Do not immediately apply extreme 'mudlark' logic unless there is torrential rain during the meeting.
handicapping_angles.midweek_metro_strength: This is Sydney's premier midweek venue. Winners of midweek Kenso handicaps possess elite form that translates very well when stepping up to Saturday Metro grade on the main Randwick or Rosehill tracks.

Nowra.bias_notes: A spacious South Coast country track featuring a long 400m home straight with a slight uphill gradient.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extremely fair. The long, uphill straight gives backmarkers plenty of time to wind up.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside draws are an advantage from the 1000m sprint start.

Rosehill_Gardens.bias_notes: Premier 'On-Pace' track despite long straight. Specialists thrive here.

main_turf.bias_notes: A very tight, circular track. With one of the shortest straights in Australian metropolitan racing (280m), this is a heavily leader-dominated circuit. 'Jump and run' tactics are essential here.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are a massive advantage. Horses drawn wide are frequently forced to expend too much early energy to cross, or are trapped wide on the tight turns.

handicapping_angles.the_night_racing_specialist: Canterbury frequently hosts Friday night racing. Some horses thrive under the lights while others get agitated. Upgrade proven night-racing performers.
handicapping_angles.on_pace_necessity: Downgrade deep closers (backmarkers) heavily. Unless the pace collapses completely, it is mathematically incredibly difficult to make up 4+ lengths in a 280m straight.
handicapping_angles.rail_position_shifts: When the rail is moved out past 3m, the inside speed bias becomes even more extreme.

Warwick.bias_notes: A regional country track in Queensland. It is a relatively tight circuit.

Esperance.bias_notes: A coastal WA regional track featuring a tight layout and a short home straight.

Warwick_Farm.bias_notes: Mid-size track. Triangular shape. On-pace bias.

Hawkesbury.bias_notes: A premier provincial circuit that often attracts elite Sydney metro stables.

handicapping_angles.class_factor: Sydney metropolitan form lines are very strong here.

Kingscote.bias_notes: Home of the famous Kangaroo Island Cup. Unique country circuit.

handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside barriers are a distinct advantage.

Geraldton.bias_notes: A spacious regional track featuring a long 400m home straight. Coastal winds dictate pattern.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Inherently fair due to the long straight.
handicapping_angles.wind_factor: CRITICAL. Wind direction decides the race.

Canberra.bias_notes: Course Proper (Turf) is spacious. Acton Track (Synthetic) favors on-pace.

Newcastle.bias_notes: Huge, spacious track. Very fair. Form translates to Randwick.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A highly regarded, modern country track featuring a perfectly symmetrical oval shape. The 1,750m circumference and generous 400m home straight make it one of the fairest galloping tracks on the New South Wales country and provincial circuits, giving all running styles a legitimate chance.
main_turf.post_bias: Extremely fair. The track utilizes two massive chutes for the 1000m and 1600m starts, which provide long, uninterrupted straight runs before the first corner. Wide barriers are rarely a severe penalty here compared to tighter NSW country tracks.

handicapping_angles.the_fairway_swoop: Because the track is so spacious and features a massive 400m straight, one-dimensional 'Need-the-Lead' front runners often find themselves exposed in the final furlong. Upgrade 'Swoopers' (closers) with sustained galloping strides.
handicapping_angles.the_chute_allowance: In 1000m and 1600m events, do not aggressively downgrade horses drawn wide. The chutes provide a massive runway to balance up and find cover without burning excessive early energy.
handicapping_angles.country_championships_quality: As a premier NSW country racing hub, Goulburn attracts high-quality regional horses as well as elite Sydney metropolitan trainers looking for confidence-boosting maiden or class 1 wins. Respect Metro-grade shippers heavily.

Thangool.bias_notes: A regional country circuit. Tight bush track.

York.bias_notes: A fast-paced country circuit located east of Perth.

Quirindi.bias_notes: A challenging, undulating country track with tight turns.

nicknames: The Heart of Victoria's Thoroughbred Country

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. Reconstructed and widened to 23 metres, Seymour is a highly regarded, spacious, and fair track. The 380m home straight gives swoopers every possible chance, while the sweeping turns allow for momentum building.
main_turf.post_bias: Generally very fair due to the long runs into the first turn, especially from the 1100m to 1300m sprint chutes which provide a massive straight run before the bend. Wide barriers are far less of a penalty here than at tighter Victorian country tracks.

handicapping_angles.the_fair_track_swoop: Because the track is expansive and the straight is 380m long, one-dimensional front-runners often tire. Heavily upgrade 'Swoopers' (closers) with strong late sectionals.
handicapping_angles.the_chute_advantage: In 1100m, 1200m, and 1300m sprints, do not heavily penalize wide draws. The chute starts provide a very long, straight run before cornering, allowing jockeys plenty of time to slot in and find cover without burning early energy.
handicapping_angles.metro_trainer_targets: Given its proximity to Melbourne and premium surface, elite Metro trainers (Hayes, Freedman, Maher) frequently send high-quality progressive horses here. Respect these runners heavily when dropping from Metro midweek or Saturday grade.

Terang.bias_notes: A spacious country track with sweeping turns.

Kembla_Grange.bias_notes: Sister track to Randwick. Long straight.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A notoriously tight, turning circuit with a very short 250m straight. It heavily favors on-pace runners and horses that can corner sharply. Deep closers are at a massive disadvantage as they simply run out of runway to wind up.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are a massive advantage, particularly in sprints (1000m, 1100m, 1200m). Horses drawn wide must expend significant early energy to cross over, or they risk being caught wide on the tight, continuous turns.

handicapping_angles.the_tight_track_bias: Aggressively upgrade early speed ('E' types) and rail-skimming pressers. Heavily downgrade pure 'Swoopers' (closers) unless they have drawn an inside barrier and the early pace is projected to be suicidal.
handicapping_angles.the_sydney_raiders: As a premier NSW Provincial track located near Sydney, elite Metropolitan trainers (Waller, Cummings, Waterhouse/Bott) frequently raid this track. Always respect horses dropping back from midweek Metro grades (like Canterbury or Kensington).
handicapping_angles.horses_for_courses: Because the geometry is so tight, large-striding horses often become unbalanced here. Heavily upgrade smaller, agile horses with proven winning form specifically at the Gosford circuit.

Queanbeyan.bias_notes: Located just outside Canberra, this track has an 1810m circumference and a 400m home straight. It frequently utilizes a cutaway rail in the straight to assist horses caught on the fence.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Relatively fair due to the 400m straight. The cutaway rail means horses saving ground on the inside turn often get a clear run home without having to fan wide.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Fair across most distances, though tactical speed to hold a position before the cutaway is highly beneficial.

Swan Hill.bias_notes: A premium country track with a lengthy 1900m circumference and tight turns. Its standout feature is a massive 475m home straight that runs slightly uphill to the finishing post.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: The long, uphill straight is a genuine stamina test. It heavily suits horses that can finish strongly, allowing backmarkers plenty of time to wind up and swoop late.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Generally fair due to the long runs from most starting points. Horses can be well back on the turn and still win.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. An exceptionally tight, circular track with one of the shortest home straights in Australia (only 143m). Because the straight provides almost zero time to wind up, momentum and early speed are absolutely crucial. The track inherently possesses a massive bias towards front-runners and horses that race on the pace.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are a massive advantage across all starts, particularly the unique 800m and 875m dashes. Horses drawn wide (8+) are at a severe disadvantage, as they are forced to cover extra ground on the continuous tight turns with no straight to recover the lost lengths.

handicapping_angles.the_extreme_speed_bias: Heavily upgrade pure front-runners ('E' types) and rail-skimming pressers with explosive gate speed. Conversely, aggressively downgrade deep closers ('Swoopers'); if a horse is not in the first four rounding the home turn, their race is effectively over.
handicapping_angles.specialist_track_form: Because the geometry is so extreme, 'Horses for Courses' is a massive angle here. Heavily upgrade horses with proven winning form specifically at Kilcoy, and apply caution to large-striding gallopers shipping in from massive, sprawling tracks like Sunshine Coast or Eagle Farm.
handicapping_angles.the_kikuyu_cushion: The track is covered in Kikuyu grass, which provides a thick cushion and holds together reasonably well during wet weather. Wet track ratings (Soft/Heavy) here often play slightly faster than standard dirt-based provincial tracks.

polytrack.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. An all-weather synthetic Polytrack constructed inside the main turf course. Unlike older, tighter synthetic tracks, it features broad, sweeping turns and a solid 320m straight. While synthetic surfaces naturally favor horses that can save ground on the rail, the spacious layout ensures that closers get a legitimate chance if the early pace is genuine.
polytrack.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) hold a distinct advantage, particularly over the 1000m to 1200m sprint distances. Being caught three-wide without cover on a synthetic surface is generally a severe penalty because horses struggle to generate the same wide, sweeping momentum they can on grass.

handicapping_angles.polytrack_specialists: Always heavily upgrade horses with proven synthetic form (Polytrack or Tapeta). Grass ratings do not always seamlessly translate to artificial surfaces. Some horses despise the kickback, while 'synthetic specialists' will grow a leg on this surface.
handicapping_angles.the_kickback_factor: Unlike turf, the Polytrack generates a decent amount of kickback (flying material). Downgrade timid stalkers drawn inside who lack early speed and are prone to getting buried in the pack, as they often resent the kickback and drop the bit.
handicapping_angles.the_pakenham_translation: Form translates beautifully between the Pakenham Synthetic track and Ballarat Synthetic. Both are spacious Victorian Polytracks. Upgrade horses shipping between these two venues who possess strong underlying synthetic speed figures.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A somewhat uniquely shaped triangular circuit with tight, sweeping turns. Because of the tighter geometry and moderate 350m home straight, it generally plays with a distinct on-pace/leader bias. The track features a Kikuyu grass base, meaning it holds together remarkably well during wet weather compared to other country tracks.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-5) are a distinct advantage across all sprint distances (1000m - 1400m). Horses drawn wide must either expend significant early energy to cross over or risk being caught wide on the tight turns, which is a massive penalty here.

handicapping_angles.the_triangle_speed_bias: Because of the triangular layout and tight corners, pure front-runners ('E' types) and prominent stalkers have a significant edge. Heavily downgrade deep closers ('Swoopers') unless they have drawn an inside barrier and can save ground the entire trip.
handicapping_angles.wet_weather_durability: Do not aggressively apply 'mudlark' extreme wet-track logic too early here. Taree's Kikuyu matting drains and handles rain much better than standard NSW bush tracks, often racing firmer than the official rating suggests.
handicapping_angles.country_championship_form: As a key track for the Mid-North Coast Country Championships qualifiers, form from elite local trainers (like Kris Lees when he sends horses up from Newcastle) is incredibly strong. Always respect horses dropping back from Provincial (Newcastle/Wyong) grade.

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. The inner of the two Sandown circuits. While tighter than the Hillside track, it still features a generous 407m home straight. On firm ground, the track plays very fairly, allowing horses to build momentum around the sweeping turns. However, when the track becomes rain-affected (Soft/Heavy), the inside rail can quickly become a 'no-go zone', heavily advantaging horses swooping down the outside.
main_turf.post_bias: In 1200m and 1300m sprints, the turns come up somewhat quickly, meaning inside barriers hold an advantage to avoid being trapped wide. In the 1400m and 1600m events, the long straight and generous run-ins make barrier position far less critical, giving wide-drawn horses ample opportunity to find cover.

handicapping_angles.the_wet_weather_swoop: When the Sandown Lakeside track is downgraded to Soft or Heavy, immediately look for 'Swoopers' (closers) drawing middle-to-outside barriers. The inside lanes frequently chop out, creating a massive advantage for horses making their runs down the center of the track.
handicapping_angles.the_hillside_translator: Sandown Lakeside is tighter than the outer Hillside circuit. Large, long-striding gallopers who thrive on the massive expanses of Flemington or Sandown Hillside can sometimes struggle with the sharper bends here. Upgrade agile, tactical horses dropping back from Hillside to Lakeside.
handicapping_angles.midweek_metro_class: As Melbourne's premier midweek workhorse track, form out of Sandown Lakeside BM64 and BM70 races is extremely strong. Horses winning here frequently measure up well when transitioning to Saturday Metro grade at Flemington or Caulfield.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A tight, narrow provincial track (only 18m wide) that is almost continuously turning. Because of the StrathAyr base, it drains exceptionally well and frequently races on firm surfaces. The tight geometry and short 310m home straight heavily favor on-pace runners who can secure the inside rail.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) hold a significant advantage. Because the track is so narrow and constantly turning, horses drawn wide are forced to cover excessive extra ground and often struggle to cross over without expending massive energy.

handicapping_angles.the_strathayr_drainage: Because of its elite StrathAyr profile, Mackay handles rain far better than standard Queensland provincial tracks. Do not over-penalize form or aggressively upgrade 'mudlarks' based solely on local wet weather forecasts unless the rain is torrential and sustained.
handicapping_angles.tight_turning_bias: Aggressively downgrade deep closers ('Swoopers'). The 310m straight and constant turns make it incredibly difficult to loop the field. Heavily upgrade front-runners ('E' types) and rail-skimming pressers.
handicapping_angles.northern_qld_raiders: Mackay sits on the primary North Queensland coastal circuit. Form lines from Rockhampton and Townsville translate well, but apply a strict class penalty to horses shipping in from smaller surrounding bush/dirt tracks (like Bowen or Home Hill).

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A highly regarded New South Wales country circuit. The track features a well-cambered turn from the 800m mark, which assists horses making wide runs. However, the relatively short 360m home straight generally rewards horses that race on or near the pace.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers hold a tactical advantage, particularly in sprint races. The kikuyu grass surface handles racing well, but leaders who can dictate terms entering the straight are incredibly tough to run down.

handicapping_angles.the_cambered_swoop: While it is largely an on-pace track, the positive camber on the home turn allows well-balanced horses to build momentum from the back. Do not entirely dismiss closers if the early pace is heavily contested.
handicapping_angles.country_championship_hub: Tamworth is a major regional hub. Respect horses trained by elite local stables or horses dropping back from provincial grades (like Newcastle or Wyong).
handicapping_angles.the_kikuyu_durability: The kikuyu surface provides a good cushion and holds together well in moderate rain, often racing better than its official wet track rating.

Hobart.bias_notes: Premier track in southern Tasmania. Features a slightly uphill 350m home straight.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Generally fair, but the uphill straight can test stamina late. On-pace runners can be advantaged when the rail is out.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Tasmanian premier form. Anchors evenly to mainland provincial baseline.

Narromine.bias_notes: A flat, country circuit in central-western NSW. The relatively short 300m straight means horses coming from the tail of the field often struggle to make up the required ground.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Moderate to strong on-pace bias. Look for horses with tactical speed that can settle in the first four.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside barriers are highly advantageous, especially in sprint races.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Deep regional form. Extreme class penalty applied if shipping to Metro venues.

Albany.bias_notes: A prominent regional track in the Great Southern region of WA. It features a 1600m circumference and a fair 340m home straight. Coastal weather and wind can play a massive factor here.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Plays relatively fair, though momentum is key. Horses that get rolling out wide on the turn often sustain their run.
handicapping_angles.weather_factor: Always check wind direction; a strong headwind in the straight can kill front-runners and set it up for swoopers.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Regional WA form. Requires heavy penalty when shipping to Ascot or Belmont Park.

Port_Augusta.bias_notes: South Australia's premier dirt track. The unique sand/dirt surface means course specialists thrive here. Horses making their debut on this surface often struggle significantly with the kickback.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: SPECIALIST TRACK. Proven dirt form is critical. Because kickback is severe, horses that can lead or sit just off the pace outside of the kickback hold a distinct advantage.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Deep regional dirt form. Extreme class penalty applied if shipping to turf Metro venues like Morphettville.

main_turf.bias_notes: A highly unique, 'figure-eight' style track with a notoriously short 210m home straight. The run into the home turn is downhill, causing horses to naturally speed up, but the short straight is sharply uphill.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers are highly advantageous. The sharp turns and short straight make it very difficult to loop the field from out wide.

handicapping_angles.the_mornington_specialist: Because of the downhill turn and uphill finish, Mornington is the ultimate 'horses for courses' track. Heavily upgrade horses with previous wins at this specific venue.
handicapping_angles.momentum_runners: The uphill straight stops weak front-runners dead in their tracks. Look for horses that can secure an economical run just behind the leaders (the 'box seat') and pop out at the top of the short straight.
handicapping_angles.class_drop_shippers: Horses dropping back from Metro racing at Caulfield or Sandown often find the provincial company here much easier, provided they can handle the tight turns.

Quirindi.bias_notes: A challenging, undulating country track with tight turns. Despite the tight corners, it features a fair 400m straight.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Relatively fair once they straighten up, but horses must be able to handle the undulations and tight corners to stay in contention.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Low-grade form. Extreme penalty applied if shipping to Metro.

main_turf.bias_notes: A picturesque but extremely tight provincial circuit. The 250m home straight is very short, ensuring that momentum and track position entering the final turn dictate the winner in the vast majority of races.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers are critical, particularly in sprint distances. Being caught wide on the continuous turns drains a horse's finishing burst.

handicapping_angles.the_backmarker_fade: Severely downgrade horses whose past performances show them regularly settling in the back half of the field. They will simply run out of real estate here.
handicapping_angles.tactical_speed_premium: Upgrade horses with enough tactical speed to cross the field early and secure the rail. The leader or the horse sitting outside the leader wins the lion's share of races.
handicapping_angles.metro_shipping_penalty: Form here is strictly regional. If evaluating a horse's past performance at Strathalbyn for a race at Morphettville, apply the maximum class penalty.

main_turf.bias_notes: A coastal regional circuit with tight turns and a relatively short 320m home straight. The defining characteristic of Ballina is the weather; it is heavily influenced by coastal rain systems.
main_turf.post_bias: Generally favors horses drawn low to middle. In heavy conditions, the inside rail often cuts out, and jockeys will steer toward the middle of the track in the straight.

handicapping_angles.the_mudlark_bias: Wet track form is absolutely critical here. Always check the track rating (Soft or Heavy). Upgrade proven 'swimmers' and heavily downgrade horses that only perform on Good (dry) tracks.
handicapping_angles.tight_turn_stamina: While the straight is slightly longer than extreme bullrings, deep closers still struggle to loop the field. Look for on-pace or mid-pack stalkers.
handicapping_angles.northern_rivers_circuit: Form lines from nearby tracks like Lismore and Murwillumbah translate perfectly to Ballina, but apply heavy class penalties if this form is being tested in Sydney or Brisbane metro races.

Bathurst.bias_notes: Fair surface with a decent straight.

Sapphire_Coast.bias_notes: Spacious country track with a long straight.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Fair track. Long straight suits swoopers.

Murwillumbah.bias_notes: Country track. Can be tight and favors on-pace runners.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: On-pace bias, especially when rail is out.

Dubbo.bias_notes: Country track with a short straight.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: On-pace bias due to short straight.

Grafton.bias_notes: July Carnival venue. Generally fair track.

Flemington.bias_notes: Headquarters. Wide, sweeping turns. Swoopers paradise.

Moonee_Valley.bias_notes: The Saucer. Tightest metro track. CRITICAL LEADER BIAS.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: EXTREME ON-PACE BIAS. Must be forward.

Caulfield.bias_notes: Triangular shape. On-pace bias but closers can win with momentum.

Orange.bias_notes: Towac Park is a regional track. High altitude, frequently heavy in winter.

Muswellbrook.bias_notes: Skellatar Park is a spacious, premium country track.

Wodonga.bias_notes: A relatively tight circuit. Short straight.

Wagga.bias_notes: The premier track in the Riverina region.

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. A relatively flat country track with an incredibly short 250m home straight. Because the final stretch is so brief, the track naturally creates a massive bias toward front-runners ('E' types) and horses that can race prominently on the pace. Closers ('Swoopers') must begin their runs well before the home turn, forcing them wide.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-7) hold a severe statistical advantage, particularly in the 1100m and 1200m sprint events. Horses drawn wide (barrier 9+) face a significant disadvantage, as they either burn too much energy crossing over or are forced to cover extra ground on the turns.

handicapping_angles.the_short_straight_trap: Aggressively downgrade deep closers. With only 250 metres of straight runway, horses settling in the back half of the field will almost always run out of ground, even if a pace duel occurs up front.
handicapping_angles.the_gippsland_heavy: Like many tracks in the Gippsland region, Moe frequently races on deeply rain-affected surfaces (Heavy 8 to Heavy 10) during the Victorian winter. Heavily upgrade proven 'mudlarks' who have documented success on genuinely heavy going, as winter races here often become a slog.
handicapping_angles.barrier_bias_penalty: Apply a strict speed-figure penalty to horses drawn wide (outside barrier 9) in sprint races. The ground loss on this tight track layout is highly detrimental to finishing positions.

Cranbourne.bias_notes: Large, spacious track but long turns can trap horses wide.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Fair, but on-pace favored when rail is out.
The uphill finish tests weak leaders.

Clare.bias_notes: A picturesque provincial track in South Australia.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: On-pace bias is prevalent due to the tight nature of the track.

Kyneton.bias_notes: Tight track with a short straight.

Port_Macquarie.bias_notes: A coastal provincial track with a 1600m circumference.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Slight on-pace bias due to the tighter corners.

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. An outstanding, expansive provincial track. It features wide, sweeping turns and a generous home straight. It is universally regarded as one of the fairest tracks in Australia, giving every horse (leaders and deep closers) a legitimate chance.
main_turf.post_bias: Incredibly fair overall. The only slight disadvantage is for horses drawn very wide (10+) in the 1100m and 1200m sprint chutes, as they can occasionally get caught 3-wide without cover on the sweeping turn.

handicapping_angles.the_fair_track_factor: Because Sale is so spacious, one-dimensional front-runners who rely on tight turns to steal races are highly vulnerable here. Heavily upgrade 'Swoopers' (closers) and horses with long, sustained galloping strides.
handicapping_angles.the_metro_raid: Elite Melbourne metropolitan trainers (from Caulfield, Cranbourne, and Pakenham) constantly send their promising horses to Sale for 'easy' provincial kills. Always respect horses dropping from Metro midweek grades into Sale handicaps.
handicapping_angles.winter_heavy_form: During the Victorian winter, Sale can become incredibly deep and heavy. When the track is rated Heavy 8 or worse, look for proven 'mudlarks' and downgrade horses whose only wins are on Good (dry) surfaces.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A sprawling, expansive galloping track and the premier venue in Queensland. The massive 2,027m circumference and sweeping turns allow large-striding horses to maintain their momentum. The defining feature is the 434m home straight, which has a subtle, exhausting uphill incline from the 800m mark to the winning post. This gradient heavily tests stamina, frequently allowing deep closers ('Swoopers') to run down tiring leaders in the final 100m.
main_turf.post_bias: In sprints (1000m to 1200m), middle-to-wide draws (5-8) often fare surprisingly well as riders have plenty of time to find cover and avoid early speed duels. In route and staying races (1600m+), inside barriers (1-4) hold a significant advantage by saving ground around the long, sweeping turns.

handicapping_angles.the_uphill_stamina_test: Heavily downgrade pure 'Need-the-Lead' sprinters, particularly those shipping in from tighter tracks like Doomben. The 434m uphill straight exposes horses lacking genuine stamina, making this one of the best tracks in Australia to back 'Swoopers'.
handicapping_angles.the_doomben_flip: Eagle Farm is directly across the road from Doomben, but they play completely differently. Doomben is a tight front-runner's track; Eagle Farm is a sprawling closer's track. Aggressively upgrade horses who ran well late at Doomben but ran out of room, as they will relish the massive open spaces of Eagle Farm.
handicapping_angles.big_striding_gallopers: Large, long-striding horses who often become unbalanced on tight provincial circuits (like Kilcoy or Ipswich) absolutely thrive here. Upgrade horses described in form guides as having a 'bounding' or long-reaching action.

Rockhampton.bias_notes: Callaghan Park features one of the longest straights in Australia (600m).

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Fair due to the long straight.

Warrnambool.bias_notes: Home of the famous May Racing Carnival. Features a relatively tight circuit with a short 305m home straight. The track can become extremely heavy during winter.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Moderate to strong on-pace bias. The short straight makes it difficult for deep closers to make up ground.
handicapping_angles.weather_factor: Wet track form is absolutely critical here, especially from May through August. Heavy track specialists thrive.

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. A relatively flat and spacious country circuit. The sweeping turns and solid 350m home straight generally provide a very fair racing surface, giving all run styles a legitimate chance.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside to middle barriers (1-6) are preferred, particularly in the 1000m to 1200m sprints, as jockeys need to secure a position quickly before navigating the sweeping bends.

handicapping_angles.country_fairness: Unlike tighter bush 'bullrings', Donald's spacious layout means backmarkers are not severely disadvantaged. Do not immediately cross out 'Swoopers' (Closers) if the early pace scenario is projected to be fast.
handicapping_angles.regional_form_lines: Form from similar Wimmera/Mallee regional tracks like Murtoa, Horsham, or Stawell translates perfectly here. However, apply strict class penalties if evaluating a Donald winner stepping up to Metro (Caulfield/Flemington) or Premium Provincial (Ballarat/Geelong) grade.
handicapping_angles.summer_firming: During the warmer months, the track often gets quite firm (Good 3 or Good 4). In these conditions, 'jump and run' on-pace horses (E types) gain a slight advantage as it becomes harder for closers to make up ground on the firm surface.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A magnificent, expansive track that is universally considered one of the fairest in New South Wales. The sweeping turns and long 400m straight ensure that every horse gets their chance, whether leading or swooping from the back.
main_turf.post_bias: Because of the wide, sweeping nature of the track and long runs to the first turn in most distances (especially the 1100m and 1300m chutes), outside barrier draws are far less penalizing here than at standard NSW country tracks.

handicapping_angles.the_hunter_valley_premium: Because Scone is surrounded by elite breeding farms, local trainers (like Paul Messara) often debut highly touted, incredibly well-bred horses here. Always respect first-starters from elite local yards.
handicapping_angles.swooper_friendly: Unlike tight NSW tracks like Canterbury or Wyong, Scone’s 400m straight allows deep closers to build through their gears. Heavily upgrade horses with strong late sectionals; pure front-runners are often run down in the final 50m.
handicapping_angles.carnival_form_translation: During the Scone Cup Carnival (May), the form is effectively Metropolitan grade. Horses that perform well here during the carnival should be given minimal class penalties when shipping to Randwick or Rosehill.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Relatively fair, but downhill run into final turn.

Wyong.bias_notes: Tight, turning track with a very short straight.

Lismore.bias_notes: A tight, turning track often affected by rain.

Gold_Coast.bias_notes: Tight track. Magic Millions venue. On-pace bias.

Balaklava.bias_notes: Known as the 'Flemington of South Australia'.

main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A notoriously tight track with a very short 300m home straight. The track features a long, sweeping turn starting from the 1000m mark all the way into the short straight. This geometry creates a massive on-pace and front-runner bias. Closers frequently run out of ground.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are an immense advantage. Horses drawn wide are forced to either burn significant early energy to cross over or risk being caught wide on the continuous sweeping turn, which is a massive penalty here.

handicapping_angles.the_bundamba_speed_bias: Heavily upgrade pure early speed ('E' types) and rail-skimming tactical stalkers. Aggressively downgrade deep closers ('Swoopers'), as the 300m straight rarely gives them enough time to reel in the leaders.
handicapping_angles.specialist_track_form: Because the shape is so unique and tight, 'Horses for Courses' is a massive angle. Heavily upgrade horses with proven winning form specifically at Ipswich, as large-striding gallopers often fail to handle the continuous corners.
handicapping_angles.provincial_metropolitan_bridge: Ipswich frequently hosts strong provincial fields and the famous Ipswich Cup. Top-tier Ipswich form often measures up well when horses step up to midweek Metropolitan grade at Doomben due to similar tight-turning characteristics.

Geelong.bias_notes: A spacious, premium provincial circuit. Features broad, sweeping turns and a generous 380m home straight, making it a very fair galloping surface.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extremely fair. The long straight gives backmarkers plenty of time and room to wind up and swoop.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Relatively fair across most distances due to the wide track layout, though inside draws help in shorter sprints.

Murray_Bridge.bias_notes: A massive, spacious provincial circuit. Features sweeping turns and a very long home straight, making it a premium and fair galloping surface.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extremely fair. The wide layout and long straight give backmarkers plenty of time to swoop, ensuring the best horse usually wins.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Very forgiving across most distances due to the large circumference and generous runs into the first turn.

Werribee.bias_notes: A tight provincial circuit located southwest of Melbourne. It features a very short 275m home straight, making it a classic 'horses for courses' track where momentum is key.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Strong on-pace bias. Because the straight is so short, horses settling in the back half of the field find it incredibly difficult to reel in the leaders.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside barriers are highly advantageous, allowing horses to save ground and secure a forward position before the short run home.

Coffs_Harbour.bias_notes: A spacious coastal track with a 1630m circumference and a generous 420m home straight. It generally plays very fairly due to the long run home, giving swoopers plenty of time.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extremely fair track. The 420m straight provides every horse a chance, ensuring closers and backmarkers are rarely severely disadvantaged.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Fair across most starting points, though inside draws are slightly beneficial from the 1000m and 1200m chute starts.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Spacious regional country form. Requires standard penalty when shipping to Metro.

Sunshine_Coast.bias_notes: Corbould Park. Big, spacious, galloping track.

Townsville.bias_notes: Provincial track. Sweeping turns but short home straight.

Ascot.bias_notes: Uphill straight tests stamina. 'Fremantle Doctor' wind aids leaders.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Generally fair but speed has edge.

Echuca.bias_notes: A flat, fair country track located on the Murray River. It features sweeping turns and a reliable 350m home straight that gives all runners a balanced opportunity.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Generally plays highly fair. On dry days, horses with tactical early speed hold an edge, but the layout doesn't unfairly penalize backmarkers.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside barriers are moderately helpful over the shorter sprint distances (1000m - 1200m) due to the run up to the first turn.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Baseline country form. Standard equalization applies.

Moree.bias_notes: A spacious, completely flat country circuit. Despite having a long 400m home straight that theoretically gives swoopers time, the local regional fields typically play heavily to forward-placed runners on top of the ground.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Slight on-pace bias despite the long run home. Deep backmarkers can find it tough to reel in fit, rolling country front-runners.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Relatively fair across middle distances, but drawing low helps navigate the early positioning phases in tight sprints.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Deep regional country form. High penalty required if shipping to Metro or Tier 2 Provincial venues.

Bunbury.bias_notes: A tighter, provincial circuit compared to Ascot.

Belmont_Park_WA.bias_notes: Winter track. Handles wet well. Fairer than Ascot.

Morphettville.bias_notes: Two tracks: Main and Parks. Main track is fair and spacious. Parks track is tighter.

Gawler.bias_notes: Undulating track with a slight uphill run home.

Pakenham.bias_notes: A modern, wide track with a massive 480m straight.

Launceston.bias_notes: Night racing venue. Short straight makes it hard to make up ground.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Strong on-pace bias due to short straight.

Happy_Valley.bias_notes: TIGHT TRACK with SHORT STRAIGHT. Strong on-pace/leader bias.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: STRONG ON-PACE BIAS. Must be forward.

Tokyo_Racecourse.bias_notes: VERY FAIR track - longest straight in Japan. Closers can win from anywhere.

Urawa.bias_notes: A small NAR dirt track featuring a very tight, flat oval.

Nakayama_Racecourse.bias_notes: TIGHT TRACK with SHORT STRAIGHT. Strong on-pace bias.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: On-pace bias. Uphill finish tests stamina.

Nagoya.bias_notes: Relocated to Yatomi in 2022. The new track features a longer 300m home straight and wider turns compared to the old Nagoya bullring. While still a dirt track, the new layout is much fairer to closers.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Early speed and on-pace runners still hold an inherent advantage on the dirt, but the longer stretch means outside swoopers now have a legitimate chance to run them down.
handicapping_angles.kickback: Standard dirt kickback applies. Inside horses must break sharply to secure position and avoid getting shuffled back into the dust.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Regional NAR dirt form. Significant penalty applied if shipping up to premier JRA venues.

main_dirt_outer.bias_notes: Left-Handed. A unique dirt oval featuring 'spiral curves'—the angles are wider entering the corners and tighter exiting them. Because of this geometry, horses are naturally forced to slow down and drift outward as they exit the turns. This frequently causes wide-drawn horses to lose significant ground and momentum. The 308m straight is fair, but the cornering dynamics heavily favor horses that can hug the inside rail.
main_dirt_outer.post_bias: Inside barriers hold a distinct advantage across all distances because of the spiral corners. Horses caught three-wide exiting the tight bends bleed massive amounts of momentum.

inner_dirt.bias_notes: Left-Handed. The inner dirt circuit is tighter overall but shares the exact same 308m home straight. Tactical speed and early positioning are highly prioritized here.
inner_dirt.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) are highly advantageous. Wide draws are a severe penalty.

handicapping_angles.the_spiral_corner_trap: Aggressively downgrade horses drawn wide who lack the early explosive speed to cross over. The 'spiral' nature of the turns means that horses caught on the outside will be slung even wider upon exiting, resulting in brutal ground loss.
handicapping_angles.the_nar_deep_sand: Like most regional NAR tracks, the surface is a deep, heavy sand that demands grueling stamina. Do not blindly translate fast, firm dirt form from JRA tracks; heavily upgrade horses with proven grinding ability over this specific deep sand profile.
handicapping_angles.night_racing_specialists: Funabashi is famous for its 'Heartbeat Nighter' events under the lights. Upgrade horses with proven form under artificial lighting, as some horses react poorly to the visual shadows on the track.

main_dirt_outer.bias_notes: Right-Handed. A sprawling, flat dirt oval. The surface is composed of 70mm of loose cushion sand over volcanic ash, making it significantly deeper, slower, and more stamina-draining than traditional North American dirt tracks. Because of the long 386m homestretch, one-dimensional front-runners often run out of gas, making it a very fair track for closers.
main_dirt_outer.post_bias: In the standard 2,000m route races, horses start in the homestretch and have a massive 500m run into the first turn. Because of this long run-up, outside barriers are rarely a severe penalty, and the track generally plays incredibly fair across all posts.

inner_dirt.bias_notes: Right-Handed. The inner oval is tighter with a significantly shorter straight (286m). Unlike the outer course, this configuration heavily favors tactical early speed and rail-skimming trips.
inner_dirt.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) hold a distinct advantage here due to the sharper turns and shorter home straight.

handicapping_angles.the_deep_sand_stamina: Never handicap TCK using traditional American speed logic. The deep sand surface demands grueling stamina. Heavily downgrade pure 'speed' horses that lack proven endurance, and upgrade grinding 'First-Over' types who can sustain a long, heavy gallop.
handicapping_angles.the_jra_invader_tax: Elite JRA (Japan Racing Association) horses frequently ship into TCK for massive stakes races. While they usually possess a massive class advantage, apply caution if they have never raced on NAR deep sand. Some brilliant JRA dirt horses completely fail to handle the heavier, slower TCK surface.
handicapping_angles.the_outer_stretch_swoop: In route races on the 1,600m outer track, heavily upgrade deep closers ('Swoopers'). The 386m straight will mercilessly expose front-runners who set too fast a pace in the deep sand.

Kawasaki.bias_notes: A very tight, flat dirt oval with extremely sharp turns.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Extreme advantage to leaders and stalkers.
Turf: Fair. Dirt: Can favor speed but fair overall.

Busan.bias_notes: Left-handed. Sand surface. Long straight allows closers.

straight_course.bias_notes: A straight galloping track used for races from 5f up to 1 Mile. There are no bends, but the topography features a downhill gradient for the first 2 furlongs before hitting a punishing 73-foot uphill climb over the final 2 furlongs to the winning post. This gradient frequently exhausts front-runners who set too fast a pace, heavily rewarding patient stalkers and deep closers.
straight_course.post_bias: Draw bias is heavily dictated by track condition. On Good/Firm ground, a 'Golden Highway' often forms on the near/stands side, massively advantaging High numbers (Stalls 20-32). When the ground is Soft/Heavy, the bias violently flips as Low numbers (far side) frequently find unpoached, better ground.

round_course.bias_notes: Right-Handed. A large, galloping triangular circuit. Despite a downhill run into Swinley Bottom, the track finishes with the same stiff uphill drag as the straight course. The turn into the relatively short home straight is sweeping, allowing prominently ridden horses to kick for home effectively, but stamina is severely tested.
round_course.post_bias: In races from 1 Mile to 1 1/4 Miles on the round course, inside (Low) draws are advantageous to save ground around the first bend. However, in large-field handicaps 1 1/2 Miles or longer, extreme low draws (1-4) are highly disadvantaged due to traffic bottlenecks entering the short run-in. Middle to high draws perform significantly better by sustaining uninterrupted momentum.

jumps_course.bias_notes: Features 10 stiff chase fences per circuit, including plain fences, open ditches, and water jumps. Regarded as a severe jumping test. Success heavily relies on jumping fluently and freewheeling economically down the Swinley Bottom descent to conserve aerobic capacity for the uphill 240-yard run-in.

handicapping_angles.the_uphill_drag: The defining characteristic of Ascot is the uphill finish. Heavily downgrade 'Need-the-Lead' front-runners in straight-course races. Pure speed is far less valuable here than the ability to sustain a long, grinding gallop.
handicapping_angles.the_golden_highway: During the summer (particularly Royal Ascot week) on fast ground, aggressively upgrade horses drawn high (stands side) in big-field straight-course handicaps (e.g., Royal Hunt Cup, Wokingham Stakes, Britannia Stakes).
handicapping_angles.royal_class_elite: During Royal Ascot, trainer angles are paramount. Apply massive upgrades to Group horses trained by elite yards (like Aidan O'Brien or John Gosden), particularly when supported in the market. Charlie Appleby's runners historically underperform market expectations as favourites in large Royal Ascot handicaps.
handicapping_angles.algorithmic_roi_upgrades: Aggressively upgrade Roger Varian favorites (+156% historical ROI) and Karl Burke/Wathnan Racing 2YOs at double-digit SPs (+202% ROI). In races over 2 miles, target the DOBbing (Double or Bust) strategy on horses priced between 2.02 and 4.25 BSP.

Newmarket.bias_notes: Rowley Mile and July Course. Wide, galloping, fair.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: The Dip can catch horses out.

York.bias_notes: The Knavesmire. Very flat, wide, galloping. Fairest track in UK.

main_turf.bias_notes: Right-handed, oval-shaped track with a steady, punishing climb over the final 3 furlongs. It is a severe test of stamina, especially when the ground is soft.
main_turf.post_bias: In sprints (5F-6F), high draw numbers (stands-side) historically hold an advantage on soft or heavy ground.

handicapping_angles.the_stamina_test: Downgrade one-dimensional speed horses (E types). Because of the uphill finish, horses dropping down in distance (e.g., from 7F down to 6F) often out-finish horses stretching out.
handicapping_angles.stands_side_rail: When the track is rated Soft or Heavy, look for horses drawn high that possess the tactical speed to grab the stands-side rail.
handicapping_angles.class_drop_shippers: Upgrade horses shipping from Tier 2 tracks like Newmarket or Ascot dropping into Class 4 or 5 handicaps here.

main_turf.bias_notes: Right-handed, pear-shaped galloping track. The final 3.5 furlongs are entirely uphill, creating one of the most testing finishes in British racing.
main_turf.post_bias: In sprints, high numbers have a distinct advantage when the ground gets soft, as runners will naturally gravitate towards the stands-side rail for better footing.

handicapping_angles.stamina_over_speed: Downgrade pure front-runners in sprints unless they have proven winning form over a furlong further. The final two furlongs will stop a tired horse dead in its tracks.
handicapping_angles.course_specialists: Because the uphill drag is so unique, heavily upgrade previous Carlisle winners. 'Horses for courses' is a massive factor here.
handicapping_angles.northern_circuit_form: Form lines from nearby stiff tracks like Pontefract or Beverley translate well here.

main_turf.bias_notes: Right-handed, relatively flat provincial track with sweeping turns. It generally plays fair, but momentum is key entering the 400m straight.
main_turf.post_bias: Low barriers (inside posts) are generally advantageous over shorter trips (up to 1600m) to save ground on the continuous turning layout.

handicapping_angles.tactical_speed_premium: Unlike stiff UK tracks, tactical early speed is rewarded here due to the flat nature of the straight. Upgrade 'P' (Presser) types who sit just off the leaders.
handicapping_angles.parisian_shippers: Classic Tier 4 French provincial circuit. Be highly cautious of horses shipping down from elite Parisian tracks (Longchamp, Chantilly, Saint-Cloud) unless they are dropping significantly in class and have shown recent fitness.
handicapping_angles.turn_momentum: Deep closers ('S' types) can struggle if they are forced 4 or 5 wide on the final sweeping turn into the straight.

main_turf.bias_notes: A right-handed, flat, oval track. It is the premier racing venue in the South-West of France. Generally very fair with a sweeping final turn and a solid 400m straight.
main_turf.post_bias: Low barrier draws (inside posts) are a distinct advantage in races between 1400m and 2000m, as wide runners lose significant ground on the continuous bends.

handicapping_angles.south_west_hq: As a premier South-West track, local elite trainers (like J.C. Rouget or F. Rohaut) regularly target this venue. Upgrade their runners heavily when returning from tougher Parisian trips.
handicapping_angles.winter_bog: During the autumn and winter months, the track can become extremely deep and holding ('Lourd'). Proven heavy track specialists ('mudlarks') are absolutely essential in these conditions.
handicapping_angles.tactical_positioning: While the 400m straight is fair, the tight final bend often slingshots the leaders. Horses sitting in the 'box seat' (3rd or 4th on the rail) historically excel here, as deep closers can be forced very wide on the turn.

main_turf.bias_notes: A completely flat track with a highly unique figure-of-eight layout. In races over 1m 2f, horses must navigate both left-handed and right-handed turns. The 5F and 6F sprints are relatively straight but feature a slight dog-leg right.
main_turf.post_bias: In large field sprints (5F-6F), high numbers (stands-side) historically hold an advantage. When the ground is soft, the entire field will usually drift to the stands-side rail searching for better ground.

handicapping_angles.the_figure_eight_agility: Because of the sharp turns in both directions, large, long-striding horses often struggle to maintain their rhythm. Heavily upgrade smaller, nimble, athletic horses with proven form on sharp tracks.
handicapping_angles.flat_speed_advantage: Unlike the stiff, grueling uphill finishes of Carlisle or Leicester, Windsor is totally flat. On Good/Firm ground, front-runners and prominent racers ('E' and 'P' types) are extremely difficult to catch.
handicapping_angles.monday_night_specialists: Windsor is the premier track for Monday night summer racing in the UK. Certain southern-based trainers heavily target these meetings, and 'horses for courses' is a massive factor here.

Lingfield_Park.bias_notes: AW track is sharp and undulating with a downhill run.

Chelmsford_City.bias_notes: A wide, galloping track with sweeping bends.

Southwell.bias_notes: Recently converted to Tapeta. Galloping AW track.

The_Curragh.bias_notes: HQ of Irish racing. Wide, galloping, fair.

Leopardstown.bias_notes: Premier dual-purpose track. Fair galloping nature.

Longchamp.bias_notes: Grande Piste is fair with long straight. Arc venue.

Te_Rapa.bias_notes: Premier Waikato track. Left-handed. Generally fair.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Fair, but on-pace in sprint races is good.

Ashburton.bias_notes: A very large, spacious, and flat track in the South Island.

Cambridge.bias_notes: New Zealand's premier synthetic all-weather track. Because the surface is so uniform, it plays very fairly, though horses drawn wide can be forced to work extremely hard on the turns.

handicapping_angles.surface_bias: SYNTHETIC SPECIALISTS. Grass form does not always translate to the Polytrack. Look for horses with proven synthetic form or tactical speed to secure the rail.
handicapping_angles.barrier_draw: Inside barriers hold a distinct advantage, as saving ground around the synthetic turns is crucial.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Synthetic baseline form. Standard equalization applies.

nicknames: The Home of the New Zealand Cup

main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise (Left-Handed). A massive, sprawling galloping track that is widely considered the fairest in New Zealand. The huge circumference and long 400m home straight give every horse a legitimate opportunity, whether leading or swooping from the back.
main_turf.post_bias: Because of the sheer size of the track and long runs to the first turn, outside barrier draws are far less penalizing here than at almost any other New Zealand venue.

polytrack.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise (Left-Handed). An all-weather alternative surface. While synthetic tracks often inherently feature a leader/rail bias, the generous 360m straight here provides a surprisingly fair run-in for closers compared to tighter synthetic tracks.
polytrack.post_bias: Inside barriers hold a slight advantage over the 1200m to 1400m synthetic trips, as saving ground around the turns is mathematically crucial on artificial surfaces.
