handicapping_angles.the_swooper_paradise: The massive 400m turf straight functions much like Flemington in Australia. Heavily upgrade deep closers ('Swoopers') who possess elite late sectionals. One-dimensional front-runners often tire and are swallowed up in the final 100m.
handicapping_angles.cup_week_class: During the NZ Cup Carnival in November, Riccarton hosts elite Group 1 racing, drawing the best from the North Island. Horses that perform well here against deep, high-class fields should carry heavily upgraded form when returning to standard South Island provincial tracks.
handicapping_angles.synthetic_specialists: When racing is moved to the Polytrack, always upgrade horses with proven synthetic form or tactical early speed. Grass ratings do not always seamlessly translate to the artificial surface.

Manawatu.bias_notes: A rectangular track with uniform turns and a 400m straight.

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Fair track. In dry conditions, on-pace runners have a slight advantage.

Te_Aroha.bias_notes: A large, spacious track featuring one of New Zealand's longest home straights (500m).

handicapping_angles.track_bias: Despite the massive straight, statistics often show a strong advantage for on-pace runners.

main_track.bias_notes: A massive 1-mile oval. Widely considered the fairest track in harness racing. The broad sweeping turns and long 985-foot stretch mean horses can win from almost anywhere, allowing deep closers a legitimate chance to swoop the field.
main_track.post_bias: Extremely fair. Outside posts (8-10) are a disadvantage, but much less severe than on 1/2 or 5/8-mile tracks because the long straightaways give drivers ample time to slot in and find cover.

handicapping_angles.the_class_epicenter: This is the premier class hub. When a horse ships out of the Meadowlands to race at smaller regional tracks (like Yonkers, Pocono, or Harrah's Philadelphia), they possess a massive class edge. Conversely, regional horses shipping in frequently struggle with the mile distance and intense fractional speed.
handicapping_angles.the_long_stretch_swoop: Heavily upgrade horses with elite final-quarter speed (e.g., sub-26.0 seconds). The long stretch exposes front-runners who lack pure stamina, and 'first-over' grinding trips are less punishing here than on tighter tracks.
handicapping_angles.pace_meltdown_potential: Because drivers know the track is fair, early speed duels are common. Always look for deep closers when the early pace projects to be contentious.

nicknames: The Home of the Flying Turns

main_track.bias_notes: A highly unique half-mile track famous for its heavily banked 'Flying Turns'. These banked turns allow horses to sustain their momentum while racing two or three-wide much better than flat half-mile tracks. It also features an open stretch (passing lane).
main_track.post_bias: Inside posts (1-4) are highly advantageous, but because of the banked turns, outside posts are not an absolute death sentence compared to tracks like Yonkers. Post 8 requires extreme gate speed to overcome.

handicapping_angles.the_passing_lane_pocket: Unlike Mohawk or Yonkers, Northfield HAS an open stretch. Heavily upgrade horses with high early speed drawn inside that can secure the 'pocket' (2nd on the rail), as they are guaranteed a clear inside lane to challenge the leader late.
handicapping_angles.the_flying_turns_swoop: Because of the banked turns, do not immediately discount horses forced to race on the outside (first-over or three-wide). Wide sweepers can sustain momentum here, especially if a speed duel up front causes a pace collapse.
handicapping_angles.ohio_circuit_shippers: As the premium hub for Ohio racing, form lines here are incredibly strong. Apply class penalties to horses shipping in from smaller fair tracks or lesser regional venues.

main_track.bias_notes: A classic 1/2-mile oval featuring an open stretch (passing lane). Because it is a tight half-mile bullring, early speed and inside positioning are inherently dominant. However, the presence of the passing lane fundamentally alters stretch dynamics, providing a massive strategic advantage to the horse sitting in the 'two-hole' (pocket).
main_track.post_bias: Inside posts (1-4) hold a severe statistical advantage, with Posts 1 and 2 being incredibly powerful. Outside posts (7-8) are a massive penalty; horses drawn wide on this tight half-mile geometry are frequently caught outside on the turns and face a grueling, ground-loss trip.

handicapping_angles.the_passing_lane_pocket: The absolute best trip at Monticello is sitting right behind the leader on the rail. Heavily upgrade inside-drawn horses with tactical speed who can secure the pocket, as they can save ground around the tight turns and shoot down the passing lane in the stretch without tipping wide.
handicapping_angles.the_bullring_outside_trap: Aggressively downgrade horses drawn in Posts 7 and 8. Unless they have overwhelming early gate speed to clear the field immediately, they will likely be 'parked' outside or forced to drop to the rear, making winning mathematically very difficult.
handicapping_angles.the_yonkers_shipper: Monticello serves as a regional circuit that frequently draws class-droppers from tougher New York and Pennsylvania tracks. Always respect horses shipping in from Yonkers or Pocono Downs, as they often outclass the local daytime claiming stalwarts.

main_track.bias_notes: A classic half-mile 'bullring' track. Because the turns are tight and the home stretch is incredibly short, early gate speed and inside positioning are paramount to success.
main_track.post_bias: Posts 1 through 4 hold a massive statistical advantage. Post 8 is the 'Death Trap' with a historically abysmal win percentage (often under 5%). Horses drawn outside must have elite gate speed to cross over early, or they are effectively eliminated.

handicapping_angles.the_half_mile_bias: Heavily downgrade deep closers. If a horse cannot leave the gate quickly or secure the pocket, it is nearly impossible to loop the field 3-wide on these tight turns.
handicapping_angles.the_big_track_shipper_trap: Apply caution to horses shipping in from 1-mile (Meadowlands) or 7/8-mile (Mohawk) tracks. Even if they have higher class or speed figures, large-striding horses frequently struggle to navigate Yonkers' tight turns.
handicapping_angles.driver_aggressiveness: With no passing lane and a short stretch, heavily upgrade aggressive drivers who 'send' their horses off the gate to secure the lead or the two-hole before the first turn.

Northfield_Park.bias_notes: Inside speed bias. 'Flying Turn' helps leaders corner fast.

Monticello_Raceway.bias_notes: Classic NY half-mile bullring. Extreme inside speed bias. Posts 1-4 dominate; Posts 7 and 8 face a massive disadvantage.

handicapping_angles.post_position: Downgrade outside posts heavily unless the horse possesses elite, proven gate speed to cross over early.

par_adjustment.algorithm_note: Lower tier NY regional form. Significant penalty applied if shipping to Yonkers or Pocono.

Mohegan_Sun_Pocono.bias_notes: Compromise track. Speed usually holds but closers can win.

Harrahs_Philadelphia.bias_notes: Similar to Pocono. Speed has slight edge.

main_track.bias_notes: A massive 7/8-mile track featuring an open stretch (passing lane) and an incredibly long 1,255-foot home straight. Because of this massive runway and the passing lane, 'wire-to-wire' speed is very difficult to sustain. The track heavily rewards patient drivers who sit in the pocket or swoop late.
main_track.post_bias: Posts 4, 5, and 6 are statistically dominant. Inside posts (1-3) are highly advantageous IF the horse has enough gate speed to secure the pocket, but can be a trap for slow starters. Outside posts (8-10) are difficult, but much more winnable here than on a half-mile track due to the long stretch.

handicapping_angles.the_endless_stretch_swoop: Heavily downgrade one-dimensional front-runners. The 1,255-foot stretch is a graveyard for tired leaders. Upgrade deep closers who can draft at the back of the pack and explode in the final 1/4 mile.
handicapping_angles.the_bullring_tax: Aggressively apply the 'Bullring Tax' to horses shipping in from half-mile Ohio tracks (like Northfield or Miami Valley). Horses used to short 400-foot stretches frequently 'hit the wall' at the 7/8 pole and get swallowed by Hoosier regulars.
handicapping_angles.the_passing_lane_pocket: The absolute best trip at Hoosier Park is the 'two-hole' (pocket). Heavily upgrade horses with high tactical speed drawn inside that can secure the spot right behind the leader, as the passing lane gives them a clear, unimpeded shot in the massive stretch.

Scioto_Downs.bias_notes: Ohio speed track. Inside posts preferred.

Red_Mile.bias_notes: Clay surface. Very fast. Favors speed/closers with elite turns of foot.

Saratoga_Harness.bias_notes: Classic half-mile speed bias. Inside posts dominate.

Freehold.bias_notes: Classic half-mile speed bias. Posts 1-3 dominate.

Batavia_Downs.bias_notes: Western NY half-miler. Standard half-mile speed bias.

nicknames: The Mecca of Canadian Harness Racing

main_track.bias_notes: A massive 7/8-mile oval with sweeping, banked turns and a grueling 1,095-foot home stretch. Because there is NO passing lane (sprint lane), drivers cannot simply sit locked in the pocket hoping for an inside opening. They must pull out and engage.
main_track.post_bias: Posts 4, 5, and 6 are statistically the most dominant. The inside rail (Post 1) can sometimes be a trap if a horse lacks gate speed, as they will get shuffled back. Post 10 is incredibly difficult, but much more winnable here than on a half-mile bullring.

handicapping_angles.the_bullring_tax: Heavily downgrade horses shipping in from half-mile tracks (like Flamboro or Western Fair) who are used to short 400-foot stretches. They frequently 'hit the wall' at the 7/8 pole and get swallowed by Mohawk regulars.
handicapping_angles.first_over_grinders: Because there is no passing lane, heavily upgrade horses with proven 'First-Over' (uncovered on the outside) toughness. The ability to grind outside the leader without cover is the most valuable trait at this track.
handicapping_angles.class_drop_plunge: Mohawk features the highest purses in Canada. When a horse drops from the Preferred/Free-For-All ranks down into mid-level condition claimers, they possess a massive class edge. Do not ignore heavy class droppers here.

Menangle_Park.bias_notes: Premier track. Big, spacious. Speed holds but closers can swoop.

Tabcorp_Park_Melton.bias_notes: Victoria's HQ. Mobile starts favor speed.

Gloucester_Park.bias_notes: Perth's bullring. Leaders and pocket sitters dominate.

Albion_Park.bias_notes: Brisbane HQ. Passing lane (Sprint Lane) available.

Alexandra_Park.bias_notes: NZ North Island. Smaller track, slight on-pace bias.

Vincennes.bias_notes: Paris. Uphill/Downhill sections. Stamina test. No passing lane.

Solvalla.bias_notes: Sweden. Fast track. Volt starts common.

metadata.notes: Unified database covering Australia, USA, UK, Europe, Asia, and Global Harness with exact mathematical class adjustments.
